sanskrit_devanagari:
अथ चेत्त्वमिमं धर्म्यं सङ्ग्रामं न करिष्यसि ।
ततः स्वधर्मं कीर्तिं च हित्वा पापमवाप्स्यसि ॥ ३३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
atha cet tvam imaṁ dharmyaṁ saṅgrāmaṁ na kariṣyasi |
tataḥ sva-dharmaṁ kīrtiṁ ca hitvā pāpam avāpsyasi || 33 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (IAST text body identical to vedabase)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.033 = MBh_06,024.033). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: If, however, you do not perform your religious duty of fighting, then you will certainly incur sins for neglecting your duties and thus lose your reputation as a fighter.
If, however, you refuse to fight this righteous war, abandoning your social duty and reputation, you will certainly incur sin.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Arjuna was a famous fighter, and he attained fame by fighting many great demigods, including even Lord Śiva. After fighting and defeating Lord Śiva in the dress of a hunter, Arjuna pleased the lord and received as a reward a weapon called pāśupata-astra.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.33 Atha, on the other hand; cet, if; tvam, you; na karisyasi, will not fight; even imam, this; dharmyam, righteous; samgramam, battle, which has presented itself as a duty, which is not opposed to righteousness, and which is enjoined (by the scriptures); tatah, then, because of not undertaking that; hitva, forsaking; sva-dharmam, your own duty; ca, and; kritim, fame, earned from encountering Mahadeva (Lord Siva) and others; avapsyasi, you will incur; only papam, sin.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 'imam dharmyam saṅgrāmam' (this righteous battle) with three qualifying clauses: (a) it has presented itself as a duty — i.e., it has become unavoidable through circumstance, not chosen; (b) it is not opposed to righteousness — it is positively dharmic, not merely permitted; (c) it is enjoined by scripture for the warrior class. The 'pāpam' (sin) is the consequence not of an isolated act but of refusing this specific class of duty. The kīrti loss is real but secondary; the primary loss is dharmic standing — the actor has abandoned a duty that came to them. Shankara is careful to scope this: pāpam attaches to the specific case where the battle was already a presented-duty, not to any avoidance of any conflict.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.33 If in delusion, you do not wage this war, which has started and which is the duty of a Ksatriya, then, owing to the non-performance of your immediate and incumbent duty, you will lose the immeasurable bliss which is the fruit of discharging your duty and the immeasurable fame which is the fruit of victory. In addition, you will incur extreme sin.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja sharpens 'imam' (this) — the verse names a specific battle that is already underway and is already-known to be Arjuna's duty. The phrase 'immediate and incumbent duty' (prāpta-prati-niyata) is doctrinally important: pāpa attaches not to abandoning duty in general but to abandoning a duty whose moment has already arrived. Three losses are named: the bliss-fruit of discharge, the fame-fruit of victory, and the additional sin of non-performance. Ramanuja preserves Shankara's scoping (this specific battle that has come) and adds the doership-frame: the Vedic prescription has activated; non-performance is now an active failure, not a passive abstention.

positions.rendering: this battle, which has presented itself as a duty, which is not opposed to righteousness, and which is enjoined
this war, which has started and which is the duty of a Ksatriya — your immediate and incumbent duty

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Modern translators flatten 'imam' (this — specifically) into the more general 'your religious duty.' Shankara and Ramanuja both read 'imam' as load-bearing: pāpa attaches to the specific case where the duty has already arrived, has the dharmic property, and is scripturally enjoined. The verse is not 'never abandon any duty.' It is 'do not abandon this duty, where this means a duty that has all three properties — already-arrived, dharmically aligned, scripturally enjoined.' The flattening to 'your religious duty' loses this scoping. The doctrinal stakes are real: a permissive reading of 2.33 makes Krishna sound like a generic obligation-enforcer; a careful reading makes him scope the obligation to a specific class of moments. Shankara's 'presented itself as a duty' and Ramanuja's 'immediate and incumbent' are doing this scoping work.

literal_meaning: But if you do not perform this righteous battle, then having abandoned your duty and reputation, you will incur sin.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse is the if-then form of the duty argument: if Arjuna does not perform this specific dharma-yuddha — already arrived, dharmically aligned, scripturally enjoined — he loses three things: svadharma (his duty), kīrti (his fame), and incurs pāpa (sin/demerit). Shankara and Ramanuja both emphasise the specificity of 'imam' (this) — the verse does not blanketly forbid the abandonment of any conflict; it forbids abandoning a duty already-presented under the three conditions named. The verse opens an if-then chain that runs through 2.34-2.36: 2.33 names the loss of svadharma and kīrti; 2.34 develops the kīrti loss into avyayām akīrtim (imperishable infamy); 2.35 develops the loss into peer-respect collapse before the maharathas. The block names quitting-mid-duty as a category of failure distinct from never-having-accepted-the-duty.
